sanskrit: अर्जुन उवाचकथं भीष्ममहं संख्ये द्रोणं च मधुसूदन । इषुभिः प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन ॥
hindiTranslation: अर्जुन बोले  हे मधुसूदन मैं रणभूमिमें भीष्म और द्रोणके साथ बाणोंसे युद्ध कैसे करूँ क्योंकि हे अरिसूदन ये दोनों ही पूजाके योग्य हैं ॥
englishTranslation: Arjuna said: How can I fight Bhishma and Drona with arrows in battle, O Madhusudana? They are worthy of worship, O destroyer of enemies.
essence: Respect can make necessary action feel unbearable.
speaker: Arjuna
simpleMeaning: Arjuna cannot imagine fighting his own respected teachers and elders. His hesitation is not tactical; it is moral and emotional.
simpleInsight: Some conflicts hurt because the people involved still matter to you.
detailedMeaning: On the Kurukshetra field, Krishna has just challenged Arjuna’s collapse. Arjuna answers with a deeper wound: Bhishma and Drona are not enemies in his heart, but revered teachers. His refusal to shoot reveals the conflict tearing him apart.
modernRelevance: You have to speak against a mentor who shaped you. Your hand freezes because the person in front of you is not just wrong — they are beloved.
translationLiteral: अर्जुन उवाच (Arjuna said) / कथम् (how) / भीष्मम् (Bhishma) / अहम् (I) / संख्ये (in battle) / द्रोणम् (Drona) / च (and) / मधुसूदन (O Madhusudana) / इषुभिः (with arrows) / प्रतियोत्स्यामि (shall fight) / पूजार्हौ (worthy of worship) / अरिसूदन (O destroyer of enemies)
insight: It names the paralysis that comes when duty collides with reverence.
